item.title: FortiBleed-Lynx Links, Scattered Spider Extradition, and Argo CD Repo-Server RCE
DHS HSIN Breach, Microsoft 365 Password Spraying, and Critical Adobe/Cursor Flaws
Oracle EBS CVE-2026-46817 Attacks, ARToken Phishing, and Japan Breach Disclosures
Tomcat CVE-2026-55957, Adobe ColdFusion RCE Patches, and AI Hallucinated Domain Abuse
Azure CLI Password Sprays, ClickFix Payloads, GitHub Revocation, and Citrix NetScaler CVEs
FortiBleed, Azure CLI Sprays, Adobe ColdFusion CVSS 10s, and Scattered Spider Charges
item.content:encoded:
Over 900 Oracle E-Business instances exposed to ongoing attacks — Must Read

Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite instances are exposed online as attackers actively exploit CVE-2026-46817, a critical unauthenticated HTTP takeover flaw in Oracle Payments. Oracle patched the issue in its May 2026 update and is urging customers to apply fixes immediately.

  • Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite instances have been found exposed online amid ongoing attacks exploiting CVE-2026-46817.
  • CVE-2026-46817 affects the File Transmission component of Oracle Payments in Oracle E-Business Suite and allows unauthenticated attackers with HTTP network access to take over vulnerable systems through low-complexity attacks.
  • Oracle patched CVE-2026-46817 in its May 2026 Critical Security Patch Update and urged customers to patch their systems immediately.
  • Defused said it observed exploitation attempts against Oracle E-Business honeypots over the weekend and noted that there was no known previous exploitation or public proof-of-concept code.
  • Shadowserver reported tracking around 950 Oracle E-Business Suite instances exposed online, but the article says it is unknown how many have been secured against CVE-2026-46817.

FortiBleed credential-theft campaign linked to Lynx ransomware — Must Read

Researchers linked the FortiBleed credential-theft campaign, which stole credentials from tens of thousands of Fortinet devices, to the INC and Lynx ransomware operations. The findings suggest the stolen FortiGate access was being used to support future ransomware intrusions.

  • SOCRadar linked the FortiBleed credential-theft campaign to members of the INC and Lynx ransomware-as-a-service groups after analyzing a Windows server used in the campaign’s infrastructure.
  • Researchers found that the FortiBleed operation used a custom tool called “FortiGate Sniffer” on compromised FortiGate firewalls to intercept VPN credentials and other authentication data from network traffic.
  • SOCRadar said the campaign targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls worldwide and deployed traffic sniffers on about 19,000 devices, with roughly 11,000 devices still compromised after notifications.
  • The investigation identified more than 200 additional operational servers beyond those originally associated with the campaign and roughly 500 servers used by the operation overall.
  • SOCRadar said it found persistent backdoor accounts using the username “adminin” on compromised systems and evidence suggesting the attackers exploited a previously undisclosed Nextcloud zero-day vulnerability.

PolinRider: North Korea-Linked Supply Chain Campaign Expands Across Open Source Ecosystems — Must Read

Socket researchers found the North Korea-linked PolinRider supply chain campaign has expanded beyond npm into Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions, with 162 malicious artifacts across 108 packages and extensions. The campaign compromises legitimate repositories, hides JavaScript loaders in config files or fake font files, and can deliver malware such as DEV#POPPER and OmniStealer, so affected developer environments should be treated as compromised.

  • Socket Threat Research Team identified 162 malicious release artifacts across 108 unique packages and extensions in npm, Packagist, Go modules, and Chrome extensions linked to the North Korea-associated PolinRider campaign.
  • PolinRider threat actors compromise legitimate GitHub repositories, plant obfuscated JavaScript loaders, and use Git history rewriting, force pushes, and anti-dated commits to make malicious changes appear older and less suspicious.
  • Observed PolinRider payloads are hidden either in configuration files such as vite.config.js or in fake .woff2 font files that can be executed through VS Code task files configured to run when a folder opens.
  • After deobfuscation, the loader contacts blockchain and public RPC infrastructure including TRON, Aptos, and BNB Smart Chain services, retrieves encrypted second-stage payload material, decrypts it with embedded XOR keys, and executes it with eval().
  • The article advises teams that installed affected package or extension versions to treat the environment as compromised, preserve forensic artifacts, rebuild from known-good lockfiles, rotate exposed secrets from a clean machine, and audit developer machines and repositories for hidden execution paths.

Progress Kemp LoadMaster Pre-Auth RCE Flaw Faces Active Exploitation Attempts — Must Read

Progress Kemp LoadMaster’s critical pre-auth command injection flaw CVE-2026-8037 is now seeing active exploitation attempts, though observed attacks have reportedly failed so far. With PoC details available, exposed appliances should be patched urgently.

  • eSentire's Threat Response Unit reported active exploitation attempts targeting CVE-2026-8037, a critical Progress Kemp LoadMaster vulnerability, beginning on June 29, 2026.
  • CVE-2026-8037 is an operating system command injection flaw with a CVSS score of 9.6 that can allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary commands on susceptible LoadMaster appliances.
  • Progress said the vulnerability exists in the LoadMaster API and is caused by unsanitized input that can be exploited by an unauthenticated attacker with permissions to execute arbitrary commands.
  • watchTowr Labs analyzed the flaw as involving improper null-termination in the escape_quotes() function, enabling specially crafted requests to the /accessv2 endpoint to manipulate heap memory and achieve command injection.
  • eSentire said the exploitation attempts it observed failed and produced no post-compromise activity, but warned that the availability of a proof-of-concept exploit and technical details is expected to drive further malicious activity.

Adobe Patches 7 CVSS 10.0 Flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic — Must Read

Adobe released urgent patches for multiple critical flaws in ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic, including seven CVSS 10.0 vulnerabilities that could allow arbitrary code execution. The company says there is no evidence of exploitation in the wild, but affected on-premise systems should be updated immediately.

  • Adobe released patches for multiple maximum-severity security flaws affecting Adobe ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic.
  • The ColdFusion update fixes critical and important issues that could lead to arbitrary code execution, privilege escalation, arbitrary file system read, and security feature bypass.
  • ColdFusion flaws addressed include several CVSS 10.0 vulnerabilities involving unrestricted file upload, improper input validation, and path traversal.
  • Adobe Campaign Classic flaw CVE-2026-48286 has a CVSS score of 10.0 and affects on-premise ACC v7 version 7.4.3 build 9396 and earlier on Windows and Linux.
  • Adobe said it has not found any exploits in the wild for the issues addressed in these updates.

Critical Cursor Flaws Could Let Prompt Injection Escape Sandbox and Run Commands — Must Read

Two critical Cursor flaws dubbed DuneSlide could let hidden prompt-injection instructions escape the editor’s sandbox and run commands on a developer’s machine without any user approval. The issues affect all versions before Cursor 3.0, so users should update immediately.

  • Cato AI Labs found two critical flaws in Cursor, named DuneSlide and tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, that could let a prompt injection escape the editor’s sandbox and run commands on a developer’s computer.
  • Both vulnerabilities affect every Cursor version before 3.0, and Cursor 3.0, released April 2, includes fixes for both issues.
  • The attack can be triggered by hidden instructions inside content the Cursor agent reads, such as a connected Model Context Protocol service or a web search result, without requiring a user click or approval.
  • CVE-2026-50548 abuses Cursor’s working_directory parameter to add an attacker-chosen path to the allowed-write list, while CVE-2026-50549 abuses a symlink safety-check fallback that can trust an in-project path when destination verification fails.
  • Once the sandbox is neutralized, later commands can run as the user, potentially giving control of the developer’s machine and any cloud or SaaS workspaces the editor is signed into.

Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-55957) Apache Tomcat Authentication Bypass via JNDIRealm GSSAPI Binds — Must Read

CVE-2026-55957 is a critical Apache Tomcat authentication bypass affecting JNDIRealm configurations that use GSSAPI LDAP binds, allowing pre-authentication access without the correct password. Organizations should upgrade to Tomcat 11.0.5, 10.1.37, or 9.0.101 and urgently identify any exposed directory-integrated Tomcat instances.

  • CVE-2026-55957 is an Apache Tomcat authentication bypass in JNDIRealm when it is configured to authenticate LDAP binds using GSSAPI.
  • The vulnerability is pre-authentication and can allow an attacker to authenticate without supplying the correct password, gaining access based on the affected directory-backed identity.
  • Affected releases include Apache Tomcat 11.0.0-M1 through 11.0.4, 10.1.0-M1 through 10.1.36, 9.0.0.M1 through 9.0.100, 8.5.0 through 8.5.100, and 7.0.0 through 7.0.109.
  • Apache Software Foundation has released patched versions for supported branches: Tomcat 11.0.5, 10.1.37, and 9.0.101.
  • The article recommends inventorying Tomcat instances using JNDIRealm with GSSAPI binds, restricting network access, monitoring directory logs, auditing recent access, disabling GSSAPI-authenticated binds where upgrades are not immediately feasible, and confirming fixes in third-party platforms that bundle Tomcat.

Azure CLI Password Spray Hits at Least 78 Microsoft Accounts in 81M+ Attempts — Must Read

Huntress says attackers made over 81 million Azure CLI login attempts and compromised at least 78 Microsoft accounts by using old breached credentials and the deprecated ROPC OAuth flow. The key takeaway is that MFA and Conditional Access can be bypassed if not applied to all users, cloud apps, and client app types, including Azure CLI logins.

  • Huntress warned of a massive, ongoing, automated password spray attack against Microsoft's Azure command-line interface that made more than 81 million login attempts between June 12 and June 26.
  • The campaign compromised at least 78 Microsoft accounts across 64 organizations, with targeting based on password prevalence in compromised password combo lists rather than business type or industry.
  • The activity originated largely from the IPv6 range 2a0a:d683::/32 controlled by LSHIY LLC, with some IP addresses resolving to the United States and a few to China.
  • The attackers leveraged the deprecated Resource Owner Password Credentials OAuth flow to bypass some poorly configured Conditional Access policies, including cases where MFA was not applied to all cloud apps, all users, or all client app types.
  • Huntress advised organizations to require MFA for all users, all cloud apps, and all client app types, restrict the Azure CLI application for non-admin users, and prioritize response by credential validity.

ARToken: Inside an EvilTokens affiliate panel targeting Microsoft 365 — Should Read

Cisco Talos uncovered ARToken, a sophisticated EvilTokens-linked phishing-as-a-service panel that targets Microsoft 365 with device-code phishing, token theft, PRT persistence, BEC tooling, and SharePoint exfiltration. The research shows the platform has evolved into a full post-compromise operations suite with advanced anti-analysis protections and Cloudflare Workers-based lure infrastructure.

  • Cisco Talos identified a phishing-as-a-service operator panel branded ARToken that shares infrastructure, API contracts, and operational patterns with the EvilTokens platform.
  • The ARToken panel exposes more than 80 API endpoints for device code phishing, Primary Refresh Token persistence, email access, business email compromise operations, and SharePoint exfiltration through a React-based dashboard.
  • Talos recovered two near-identical phishing messages that spoofed a legitimate Wisconsin contractor and targeted an accounts-payable recipient at a U.S. life-sciences company with an outstanding-invoice lure.
  • The ARToken phishing kit uses a seven-layer anti-analysis system that includes user-agent checks, automation detection, browser fingerprinting, interaction telemetry, timing gates, and mouse-movement pattern analysis.
  • The ARToken operator toolkit includes token refresh and sharing, Outlook inbox access, victim email sending, inbox rule creation, SharePoint and OneDrive file operations, Cloudflare Workers deployment management, and a standalone Windows session browser called ARTBrowser.

Unpatched Argo CD Repo-Server Flaw Could Let Attackers Take Over Kubernetes Clusters — Should Read

Argo CD’s repo-server has an unpatched unauthenticated RCE flaw that can let an attacker with internal network access execute code and potentially take over Kubernetes clusters. Until a fix is available, users should enable strict Kubernetes network policies to isolate repo-server and Redis from other pods.

  • Synacktiv reported an unpatched flaw in Argo CD’s repo-server component that can let an unauthenticated attacker run code if they can reach the component’s internal network port.
  • The flaw abuses kustomize’s --helm-command option through the repo-server’s GenerateManifest service to execute a script from an attacker-controlled Git repository instead of the helm binary.
  • Synacktiv demonstrated the attack against Argo CD v2.13.3 and said there is no patched release and no CVE for the issue.
  • The Helm chart for Argo CD leaves Kubernetes network policies disabled by default, which can allow an attacker who compromises a single pod in the cluster to reach the repo-server and trigger the bug.
  • The article recommends enabling Kubernetes network policies so only Argo CD’s own components can reach the repo-server and Redis ports.

Researcher Analyzes 3,000 Live ClickFix Payloads, Exposing API-Driven Malware Delivery — Should Read

ClickFix campaigns have evolved into API-driven malware delivery platforms that generate unique, obfuscated payloads for each visitor, making detection harder. Researchers also found a newer Downloads-folder method designed to bypass clipboard and script-scanning defenses, so defenders should focus on suspicious process chains rather than clipboard contents alone.

  • Security researcher Bert-Jan Pals analyzed roughly 3,000 payloads from live ClickFix campaigns and found that malicious commands are being delivered by API-driven backend servers.
  • One ClickFix server returned 100 different payload wrappers for 100 requests, using rotating combinations of Base64, AES, TripleDES, Rijndael, and Deflate while unpacking to the same script at the time of the research.
  • Newer ClickFix pages can quietly download a file to the Downloads folder and copy a short command that moves, unpacks, and runs the file, a method designed to avoid clipboard-focused detection and slip past Windows script scanning.
  • ClickFix lures are served in 25 languages, can match commands to a visitor’s operating system, and include macOS versions alongside Windows versions.
  • The article says defenders should focus on process chains such as explorer.exe or WindowsTerminal.exe launching powershell.exe, cmd.exe, or msiexec.exe and making network connections soon afterward.

Citrix Patches Six NetScaler Flaws Allowing File Read and Denial-of-Service — Should Read

Citrix released patches for six NetScaler ADC and Gateway vulnerabilities that could allow arbitrary file reads, memory overreads, or denial-of-service under certain configurations. Customers should upgrade to fixed versions immediately and, for CVE-2026-13474, ensure the HTTP/2 small-window timeout setting is configured as required.

  • Citrix released security updates for six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway that could enable arbitrary file reads or denial-of-service conditions.
  • The affected issues include CVE-2026-8451, CVE-2026-8452, CVE-2026-8655, CVE-2026-10816, CVE-2026-10817, and CVE-2026-13474, with CVSS scores ranging from 6.9 to 8.8.
  • Patches are available in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway 14.1-72.61 and later, 13.1-63.18 and later, NetScaler ADC 14.1-FIPS 14.1-72.61 FIPS and later, and NetScaler ADC 13.1-FIPS and 13.1-NDcPP 13.1.37.272 and later.
  • For CVE-2026-13474, Citrix advised customers not using HTTP Strict Profiles to manually set Http2SmallWndTimeout to 30 seconds after upgrading, because the default value is 0.
  • Citrix said there is no evidence that the vulnerabilities have been exploited in the wild.

DHS confirms hackers breached HSIN info-sharing platform — Should Read

DHS confirmed hackers breached the unclassified Homeland Security Information Network, a sensitive information-sharing platform used by government and private-sector partners. The agency says it isolated affected systems and found no indication classified networks were impacted, but it remains unclear whether documents were stolen.

  • The Department of Homeland Security is investigating a cyberattack that compromised the Homeland Security Information Network, a sensitive but unclassified information-sharing platform.
  • The intrusion was carried out by an unknown threat actor in recent weeks and is believed to have occurred sometime between late May and early June, according to Nextgov sources.
  • DHS said it isolated the affected systems, mitigated the vulnerability, and launched a comprehensive forensic investigation.
  • DHS said there is no indication that classified networks were impacted and that the system remains operational for partners.
  • It remains unclear whether any documents were stolen from the system.

Phantom Squatting Uses AI-Hallucinated Domains for Phishing and Malware — Should Read

Attackers are registering domains that AI models hallucinate and using them for phishing and malware, exploiting users’ trust in AI-generated links before reputation systems can flag the sites. Defenders can reduce risk by monitoring likely hallucinated domains and treating all AI-provided links as unverified until confirmed.

  • Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 calls the practice of registering AI-hallucinated web domains for phishing and malware "phantom squatting" and says it is already occurring in the wild.
  • Unit 42 asked two AI models 685,339 questions about 913 well-known brands, receiving 2.1 million links, including 13,229 flagged as malicious and roughly 250,000 invented domains with no owner.
  • In one observed case, an attacker registered an AI-predicted fake domain resembling a national postal service's online marketplace 23 days after Unit 42 predicted it and used the Montana Empire phishing kit to steal card numbers, bank-transfer details, and national ID data.
  • In another observed case, Unit 42 flagged a hallucinated postal-service domain 51 days before an attacker registered it, cloned a brand site, added fake credibility claims, and used it to push a malicious Android app.
  • The article advises users not to trust links just because an AI provided them, to verify official domains before entering passwords, and to prevent AI agents from automatically opening or downloading from model-generated links without checks.

The SOC Files: ScreenConnect masked as freeware. An inside look at a large-scale campaign — Should Read

Kaspersky uncovered a large SEO-poisoning campaign that uses fake freeware download sites to install legitimate ScreenConnect remote access software via DLL sideloading, then deploy AsyncRAT for persistence and control. The key risk is that trusted remote administration tools can be silently abused at scale to compromise both consumers and corporate endpoints.

  • Kaspersky MDR investigated an incident in which ScreenConnect was used to deploy and execute an AsyncRAT payload on a compromised system.
  • The investigation traced the ScreenConnect installation to a user-downloaded archive from a typosquatted OBS Studio website, studioobs.com, which appeared in search engine results.
  • The malicious archives contained a legitimate Microsoft-signed install.exe file and a rogue install.res.1033.dll library that was loaded through DLL sideloading to install ScreenConnect and the impersonated software.
  • After ScreenConnect ran, it created PowerShell and VBScript files that changed Microsoft Defender exclusions, disabled UAC prompts, loaded a decrypted PE file, injected AsyncRAT into RegAsm.exe, and created a scheduled task named MasterPackager.Updater for persistence.
  • Kaspersky identified more than 90 localized fake domains across 10 languages that impersonated popular software and used ScreenConnect command-and-control infrastructure to maintain access to compromised endpoints.

New ChocoPoC malware targets researchers via trojanized PoC exploits — Should Read

Attackers are targeting cybersecurity researchers with GitHub proof-of-concept exploits that look legitimate but install malicious PyPI dependencies delivering the ChocoPoC RAT. Researchers should treat unverified PoCs as hostile and run them only in isolated environments.

  • Multiple weaponized proof-of-concept exploits on GitHub were found delivering a Python-based remote access trojan named ChocoPoC in a campaign believed to target cybersecurity researchers.
  • ChocoPoC is delivered through malicious Python packages added to the proof-of-concept dependency list, rather than by embedding the malware directly in the exploit file.
  • Sekoia identified at least seven GitHub proof-of-concept repositories distributing ChocoPoC for vulnerabilities affecting FortiWeb, React2Shell, MongoBleed, PAN-OS, Ivanti Sentry, Check Point VPN, and Joomla SP Page Builder.
  • The skytext package used in the campaign was downloaded 2,400 times, mostly on Linux-based systems, with download spikes following disclosure of a popular vulnerability.
  • Sekoia assessed with high confidence that the attacker primarily used compromised accounts to publish malicious PyPI packages and proof-of-concept repositories.

19-Year-Old Scattered Spider Suspect Extradited to Face U.S. Hacking Charges — Should Read

A 19-year-old alleged Scattered Spider member has been extradited from Finland to the U.S. to face hacking, fraud, and extortion charges tied to multiple intrusions. The case highlights a widening law-enforcement crackdown on the social-engineering crew behind major attacks on casinos, retailers, insurers, and airlines.

  • Peter Stokes, a 19-year-old dual U.S. and Estonian citizen accused of belonging to Scattered Spider, was extradited from Finland to face U.S. charges of conspiracy, computer intrusion, and fraud.
  • Court records identify Stokes by the online handle "Bouquet" and describe at least four intrusions, including a May 2025 intrusion at a luxury jewelry retailer where attackers allegedly demanded about $8 million in cryptocurrency.
  • Scattered Spider is described as a loose, mostly English-speaking group of young people that uses help desk impersonation to gain access to company systems, steal files, and threaten leaks unless paid.
  • The group has been linked to major attacks on MGM Resorts and Caesars Entertainment in 2023 and later attacks on U.K. retailers, U.S. insurers, and airlines.
  • Recent arrests and guilty pleas involving alleged Scattered Spider members in the U.S. and U.K. show a broader law enforcement crackdown on the group.

VEIL#DROP Malware Chain Uses Blogger Platform to Deliver PureLogs Stealer — Should Read

Researchers uncovered a multi-stage malware chain called VEIL#DROP that uses deceptive JavaScript files, PowerShell, and Blogger-hosted payloads to secretly deliver the PureLogs information stealer. The campaign abuses trusted services and living-off-the-land binaries to evade detection and reduce forensic traces.

  • Securonix identified a new multi-stage malware delivery chain called VEIL#DROP that uses social engineering and Blogger pages to deliver the PureLogs information stealer.
  • The infection chain begins with a JavaScript file disguised as a document, such as transcript.pdf.js, which runs through Windows Script Host and launches PowerShell with execution policy bypasses enabled.
  • The PowerShell script retrieves a next-stage payload from a Blogger-hosted page, allowing the attackers to abuse Google’s trusted infrastructure as a staging mechanism.
  • The loader uses techniques including XOR decryption, dynamic Blogspot URL construction, runtime mutation, in-memory execution, and reflective .NET loading to evade detection and reduce disk artifacts.
  • If direct in-memory execution is blocked, the loader can fall back to Microsoft-signed binaries such as regsvcs.exe, installutil.exe, msbuild.exe, and aspnet_compiler.exe to execute the recovered .NET assemblies.

Ousaban Banking Trojan Targets Iberian Bank Users with Fake PDF Lures — Should Read

Ousaban, a Brazilian banking trojan, is targeting Windows users in Spain and Portugal with fake corrupted-PDF lures that geofence victims and hide the payload inside an image. Once installed, it waits for banking sessions to steal credentials and enable account takeover.

  • Fortinet's FortiGuard Labs identified a May 2026 campaign in which the Brazilian banking trojan Ousaban targets Windows users who bank in Spain and Portugal.
  • The attack begins with a phishing PDF disguised as a corrupted file that prompts the victim to press an "Atualizar" button, which opens a malicious webpage.
  • The campaign screens visitors so that users outside Spain or Portugal receive a Spanish "access denied" notice instead of the malware.
  • After passing the screening, a script downloads an image that looks like a PDF icon but hides a ZIP file containing Ousaban, then runs the malware and deletes evidence of the download.
  • Once installed, Ousaban waits for the user to open targeted banking sites and can capture screenshots and keystrokes, tamper with the clipboard, show fake messages, and give attackers remote control.

US lifts export controls on Anthropic’s frontier cybersecurity AI models — Should Read

The U.S. lifted export controls on Anthropic’s Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity AI models after Anthropic agreed to new safeguards, government pre-release testing, and jailbreak reporting measures. The reversal may shape how frontier AI cybersecurity tools are regulated going forward.

  • Anthropic restored global access to its Fable 5 model after the U.S. government lifted export controls that had forced a roughly three-week shutdown.
  • Export controls on Anthropic’s Mythos 5 model were also fully lifted as of June 30, but access remains limited to vetted U.S. organizations through Project Glasswing.
  • Anthropic said the initial shutdown was triggered by a jailbreak technique from an Amazon research report, but later testing found the same technique worked against other models including OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 and Kimi K2.7.
  • As part of the agreement to restore access, Anthropic trained a new safety classifier that it says blocks the specific technique in more than 99% of cases and committed to expanded government evaluation and rapid jailbreak disclosure.
  • More than 100 cybersecurity professionals signed an open letter warning that the export controls could do more harm than good by removing advanced defensive capabilities while adversaries’ models continue to improve.

AI-Generated Browser Ransomware Abuses Chromium API on Windows and Android — Should Read

Check Point researchers found AI-generated malware dubbed InfernoGrabber that uses Chromium’s File System Access API to run browser-only ransomware on Windows and Android after tricking users into granting folder access. The key concern is that LLMs like DeepSeek may lower the skill needed to discover and build novel attack chains using legitimate browser features.

  • Check Point researchers flagged a malware artifact generated using DeepSeek that combines unrealistic browser-malware concepts with a real browser capability to create a working ransomware technique running entirely inside the browser on Windows and Android devices.
  • The sample, named InfernoGrabber v9.0 by its author, is a Python Flask application uploaded to VirusTotal on January 25, 2026 and described as a fully functional information stealer and ransomware toolkit.
  • The malicious web server lures victims with a fake Discord avatar AI upscaler while including functions to steal Discord tokens, harvest credit card numbers and cryptocurrency seed phrases, log keystrokes, capture webcam and microphone feeds, and exfiltrate data through a hard-coded Discord webhook.
  • The browser-only ransomware technique tricks users into granting file system access to a web page, then enumerates files in the selected folder, reads and exfiltrates their contents, encrypts and overwrites them, and displays an extortion note without installing a native payload, exploiting a browser vulnerability, or requiring root access.
  • Check Point said the approach is limited to browsers exposing the picker-based File System Access API, including Google Chrome and other Chromium-based browsers on Windows and Android, and there is no evidence the browser-native ransomware pattern has been abused in the wild.

OpenClaw: risks for the users and how to mitigate them — Should Read

OpenClaw’s rapidly growing AI-agent ecosystem poses significant security risks because skills can execute natural-language or code-like instructions with access to sensitive files, tokens, and systems. Organizations should treat third-party skills as supply-chain threats, scan and vet them, restrict agent privileges, monitor network activity, and enforce an AI tool usage policy.

  • OpenClaw, previously known as Clawdbot and Moltbot, is a fast-growing AI agent ecosystem used by employees worldwide to automate tasks, often without awareness of the risks to themselves and their employers.
  • OpenClaw skills are plaintext instruction files, often named SKILL.md, that can reside locally or come from external sources such as ClawHub and may require access to file systems, tokens, and keys.
  • The article states that around 530 vulnerabilities have been discovered in OpenClaw and underlying technologies in less than two years, with many high-severity issues involving sensitive data storage and excessive privileges.
  • Kaspersky’s April scan of the skill hub identified 24 accounts distributing more than 600 malicious skills, and open-source intelligence indicated more than 1100 malicious accounts had been created since January.
  • The article recommends layered protection, isolating the OpenClaw agent from critical data and infrastructure, checking all skills entering an organization, monitoring the agent’s network access, and creating a comprehensive AI policy.

And another one. GitHub ships break-glass credential revocation — Should Read

GitHub has added a break-glass credential revocation feature for Enterprise customers, letting owners quickly cut off compromised PATs, SSH keys, and OAuth tokens across an organization. The move addresses recent supply-chain incidents where incomplete credential rotation allowed attackers to regain access and spread compromises.

  • GitHub released self-service credential revocation for Enterprise, allowing organization owners to revoke compromised credentials across an organization in one action.
  • The feature covers SSO authorizations for Personal Access Tokens, SSH keys, and OAuth tokens, and each action creates an audit log entry and sends email notifications to affected users.
  • The article links the need for this feature to recent incidents involving Microsoft durabletask, Aqua Security Trivy, and Checkmarx, where incomplete credential cleanup or stolen credentials enabled follow-on compromise.
  • Before this feature, cutting off one account’s credentials required using multiple tools and could depend on having SAML SSO enabled or already possessing the leaked token string.
  • The article recommends assigning the “Manage enterprise credentials” permission before an incident and pinning GitHub Actions to full commit SHAs instead of version tags.

Kubota says hackers had month-long access to network systems — Should Read

Kubota North America says hackers accessed some network systems for over a month and viewed files containing sensitive personal data of employees and dependents, including Social Security numbers, IDs, bank details, and benefits information. The company is notifying affected individuals and offering Kroll identity protection.

  • Kubota North America Corporation disclosed that hackers had access to some of its network systems between March 16 and April 20.
  • The company determined that the threat actor accessed files containing personal information for employees and their dependents.
  • Potentially exposed data includes full names, Social Security numbers, dates of birth, taxpayer IDs, government ID numbers, direct deposit bank account information, corporate payment card information, and benefits enrollment and limited claims data.
  • Kubota began sending personalized email notifications on June 30 and included instructions for enrolling in Kroll identity protection.
  • Kubota said it implemented additional security measures, and no data extortion group or ransomware gang had claimed responsibility at the time of writing.

Microsoft Accelerates Post-Quantum Cryptography Shift to 2029 — Should Read

Microsoft is accelerating its post-quantum cryptography roadmap, aiming to transition critical products and services to quantum-safe encryption by 2029 as quantum computing risks appear to be arriving sooner than expected. The move aligns with similar timelines from Google and Cloudflare and reflects growing concern over “harvest now, decrypt later” threats.

  • Microsoft is accelerating its Quantum Safe Program timeline with the goal of transitioning critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029.
  • Microsoft plans to incorporate post-quantum cryptography requirements into its Secure Future Initiative.
  • Microsoft said key focus areas include adopting TLS 1.3, building crypto-agility for stored data, and transitioning to post-quantum algorithms for trust chains such as code signing, certificate issuance, key protection, and update pipelines.
  • The article says the move follows a U.S. executive order setting deadlines for federal agencies to move high-value assets and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography.
  • Google and Cloudflare have also announced plans to move toward post-quantum cryptography by 2029.

UK Cybercrime Journal: Argos Account Takeover Fraud — Should Read

UK police warned of a sharp rise in Argos account takeover fraud, where criminals use leaked reused passwords to access accounts, place orders, and collect goods in store via Click & Collect. The key takeaway is that consumers need stronger account security like unique passwords and MFA, while retailers should add stronger collection and fraud controls for high-value purchases.

  • On 3 June 2026, the City of London Police warned that Report Fraud had seen a significant increase in fraud cases mentioning Argos.
  • Report Fraud said cybercriminals are using leaked credentials from historical data breaches to hijack Argos user accounts.
  • Fraudsters place orders from compromised Argos accounts and often collect the goods in person through the retailer’s Click & Collect option.
  • Report Fraud received 652 reports mentioning Argos in May, a 323% increase from 154 reports in April, and 1,175 such reports since the start of 2026.
  • The article recommends that users use unique passwords, password managers, passkeys, and multi-factor authentication, while retailers add stronger Click & Collect controls and credential-stuffing detection.

Where Severity Scores Go Wrong: “Just Add Prototype Pollution” — May Read

JFrog argues that many Axios prototype pollution-related CVEs are scored too severely because Axios is only exploitable when a separate prototype pollution flaw already exists in the same JavaScript runtime. The key takeaway is that severity scores should be treated as a starting point and adjusted based on real-world exploit prerequisites and application context.

  • JFrog researchers found that all 10 Axios CVEs discussed in the article require a pre-existing prototype pollution condition within the same JavaScript runtime context to be exploitable.
  • The article’s proof of concept chained the Lodash prototype pollution vulnerability CVE-2019-10744 with the Axios proxy injection gadget CVE-2026-44494 to redirect requests through an attacker-controlled server.
  • In the proof of concept, Axios consumed an attacker-controlled proxy property inherited from Object.prototype and routed outbound requests through a malicious proxy, enabling traffic interception and possible credential theft.
  • JFrog argues that the severity assigned to these Axios issues often reflects the impact of a complete attack chain rather than the standalone risk introduced by Axios itself.
  • The article states that organizations should treat CVSS and similar scores as a starting point and combine them with contextual analysis of exploit prerequisites, environmental constraints, and application-specific behavior.

Four Days Old, Fully Authenticated: CEO Coaching International Impersonation Targets a Sports Technology Company — May Read

Attackers used a four-day-old lookalike domain impersonating CEO Coaching International to send a fake overdue invoice with collections pressure to a sports technology company. Although SPF, DKIM, and DMARC passed through SendGrid, header inspection revealed the authenticated sending domain did not match the claimed sender, making domain alignment a key detection signal.

  • Attackers registered the lookalike domain ceo-coachinginc.com on March 6, 2026, and sent the campaign email to a sports-technology company on March 10, 2026.
  • The email impersonated CEO Coaching International and claimed to be from a fabricated persona named Carol Smith, CPA, with an overdue invoice and a warning that the matter would be referred to a collections agency.
  • The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, but the DKIM-signing and Return-Path domain was an unrelated SendGrid sending domain rather than ceo-coachinginc.com.
  • The Reply-To addresses included carol@ceo-coachinginc.com and admin@azur-email.com, giving the attackers two inboxes to receive responses.
  • Themis scored the message at 63% confidence, and IRONSCALES surfaced it for analyst review based on the combined signals of a newly registered lookalike domain, authentication mismatch, financial theme, and urgency language.

Amazon fined $2.25M for withholding evidence from fraud victims — May Read

Amazon will pay a $2.25 million FTC civil penalty for allegedly violating the Fair Credit Reporting Act by denying or delaying identity theft victims’ access to records of fraudulent transactions made in their names. The proposed order requires Amazon to provide lawful records requests to victims and authorized law enforcement within 30 days.

  • The U.S. Federal Trade Commission said Amazon will pay a $2.25 million civil penalty to settle charges that it blocked identity theft victims’ access to transaction records.
  • The FTC alleged that Amazon failed to provide many fraud victims with records of fraudulent transactions made in their names, as required by Section 609(e) of the Fair Credit Reporting Act.
  • Amazon customer service agents allegedly denied many consumers’ requests for records for “privacy” or “security” reasons, and Amazon sometimes provided records after the 30-day timeframe required by law.
  • The FTC said Amazon also refused to provide application and business transaction records to authorized law enforcement agencies acting on behalf of identity theft victims.
  • Under the proposed order, Amazon must provide lawfully requested records to identity theft victims and law enforcement agents within 30 days and notify certain consumers who requested records since April 2024 that they may request additional records.

StepSecurity Maintained Actions Are Now Free for Public Repos — Optional

StepSecurity is making its maintained, security-reviewed drop-in replacements for popular third-party GitHub Actions free for public repositories. The move aims to reduce open-source CI/CD supply chain risk, especially from abandoned or compromised actions like tj-actions/changed-files.

  • StepSecurity announced that its Maintained Actions are now free for public repositories, making a capability that was previously enterprise-only available to open-source projects on GitHub.
  • The article says third-party GitHub Actions can access secrets, cloud credentials, and source code in CI/CD environments, creating risk when actions are abandoned, poorly licensed, or lack basic security practices.
  • In March 2025, the tj-actions/changed-files action used by over 23,000 repositories was compromised in CVE-2025-30066, and StepSecurity’s Harden-Runner detected the incident.
  • StepSecurity Maintained Actions are open-source, API-compatible replacements for popular third-party GitHub Actions and are designed to be swapped in with a simple workflow file change.
  • StepSecurity says its maintained actions follow security controls including manual code review, limited write access, branch and tag protection, secure release processes, vulnerability patching SLAs, and monthly upstream syncs.

Secure Registry now tells you which machine pulled a compromised package — Optional

StepSecurity’s Secure Registry now attributes npm and PyPI package requests to the specific developer machine or CI workflow that made them, helping teams quickly identify where compromised packages landed. This turns supply-chain incident response from manual log hunting into a direct query across affected sources.

  • On June 17, 2026, an attacker compromised the @mastra npm organization and added easy-day-js, a typosquat of dayjs, as a dependency across more than 140 packages in the Mastra AI framework ecosystem.
  • The malicious easy-day-js version included an obfuscated postinstall dropper that downloaded a second-stage payload from an attacker-controlled server and then deleted itself.
  • StepSecurity says Secure Registry now adds Source and Source Identifier columns to its audit log to attribute each npm and PyPI request to the machine or pipeline that made it.
  • Requests from developer machines can link to a device page showing installed IDE extensions, AI agents, MCP servers, OSS packages, and suspicious files, while GitHub Actions requests can link to the corresponding Harden-Runner workflow run.
  • Secure Registry users can filter the Policy Evaluations log by package and version to identify affected machines and pipelines instead of searching scattered install logs.

2026 Cybersecurity Assessment: The Gap Between Awareness and Resilience — Optional

Bitdefender’s 2026 Cybersecurity Assessment finds that organizations understand major cyber risks like AI exposure, attack surface reduction, and breach transparency, but struggle to turn that awareness into practical resilience. The biggest gap is operational: limited visibility, resources, and governance leave teams less prepared than leaders often believe.

  • The 2026 Bitdefender Cybersecurity Assessment surveyed 1,200 IT and cybersecurity professionals across six countries and found a gap between awareness of cyber risk and operational resilience.
  • While 51.8% of respondents believe they have full visibility into sanctioned and unsanctioned AI use, 47.4% report only partial or no visibility into Shadow AI tools or personal AI accounts used for work.
  • Respondents cited maintaining hardening policies and exceptions, fear of disrupting business operations, limited resources, and uncertainty about required user tools as major obstacles to reducing the attack surface.
  • Security professionals ranked AI-related threats as their top three concerns, but Bitdefender Labs found that 84% of high-severity attacks leveraged Living off the Land techniques while only one in five respondents ranked those attacks among their top three concerns.
  • More than half of respondents who experienced a breach in the previous twelve months said they were instructed to keep the incident confidential despite believing authorities should have been notified.

Unmasking the crawls with Attribution Business Insights — Optional

Cloudflare launched the Attribution Business Insights dashboard for Bot Management customers to help website owners measure AI crawler traffic, crawl-to-referral ratios, bandwidth impact, and bot operators accessing their content. The key takeaway is that publishers can now use clearer business-focused data to decide which AI bots provide value and which should be blocked or renegotiated with.

  • Cloudflare announced the Attribution Business Insights dashboard for all Cloudflare Bot Management customers.
  • The dashboard is designed to help website owners understand which bots provide value and which bots strain resources while reducing human consumption of their content.
  • The dashboard shows bot versus human traffic, successful bot access to content pages, site-wide and per-operator crawl-to-referral ratios, top bots by volume, country of origin, bandwidth usage, and current allow or block status.
  • Cloudflare classifies crawlers by behavior as Training, Search, or Agent rather than only using a generic “AI Crawler” label.
  • Cloudflare says actions based on the dashboard’s insights still happen through Security rules, while the dashboard is intended as a filtered analytics hub for business decision-makers and other stakeholders.

Healthcare’s Governance Needs To Speed Up To Combat Unsanctioned AI Usage — Optional

Health sector organizations are seeing growing unsanctioned use of AI as formal governance and adoption processes lag behind employee demand. The key takeaway is that healthcare leaders need faster, more practical AI governance to reduce security, privacy, and compliance risks from shadow AI.

  • The article argues that healthcare governance needs to speed up to address unsanctioned AI usage.
  • Hospitals have spent the last decade modernizing their digital infrastructure.
  • Formal adoption of new tools in hospitals has been difficult because of technical and regulatory hurdles and, at times, employee resistance.
  • The article says there is a widening gap between how health sector organizations want to use AI and how AI is actually being used inside and outside their walls.
  • The article frames unsanctioned AI use as an issue involving an unofficial AI ecosystem, shadow AI risks, evolving governance, and efforts to close the gap.

Monthly Newsletter – July 2026 — Optional

Health-ISAC’s July 2026 newsletter highlights upcoming European and Americas Summit deadlines, new workshops and webinars, and several new healthcare cybersecurity resources, including research on Claude Mythos and guidance on third-party IAM. The key takeaway is that Health-ISAC is emphasizing preparation for emerging AI-driven cyber risks while promoting upcoming community events and security resources.

  • The July 2026 Health-ISAC newsletter highlights the 2026 Health-ISAC European Summit, scheduled for October 12–16, 2026, in Crete, Greece, with early bird registration and subsidized hotel accommodations.
  • The newsletter announces a Friday post-Summit excursion to Knossos Palace with an expert guide and a seaside lunch featuring Cretan cuisine.
  • The 2026 Health-ISAC Fall Americas Summit, titled “Orbit of Trust,” is scheduled for November 30–December 4, 2026, in Tucson, Arizona, with abstract submissions closing on July 10 and registration opening on September 9.
  • Health-ISAC and Quest Diagnostics conducted joint research on Claude Mythos, an Anthropic AI model described as having autonomous zero-day vulnerability discovery and weaponization capabilities.
  • The newsletter points to new resources including a Health-ISAC white paper on managing third-party IAM, a Human Risk Management Maturity Model report by Living Security, and an on-demand webinar by Ridge Security on new vulnerabilities aimed at the healthcare industry.

Japanese insurer, brewer, manufacturer and telecom disclose cyber breaches — Optional

Several major Japanese companies, including Aflac Japan, Sapporo subsidiaries, Nidec and KDDI, disclosed separate cyber incidents involving exposed customer data, disrupted systems and ongoing investigations. The largest known breach affected Aflac Japan, where personal data for about 4.38 million policyholders was exposed.

  • Several major Japanese companies disclosed cyber incidents over the past two weeks, and the article says there is no evidence so far that the attacks are connected or were carried out by the same threat actor.
  • Aflac Life Insurance Japan said hackers compromised its customer portal and other systems, exposing personal information for about 4.38 million policyholders, including names, addresses and phone numbers.
  • Aflac Japan said about 230,000 customers also had their premium payment account information compromised, while the incident was limited to its Japanese operations and did not affect its U.S. business.
  • Sapporo Holdings disclosed suspected unauthorized access affecting Singapore-based Pokka and Canadian brewer Sleeman Breweries, and said it shut down affected systems while investigating whether any data was stolen.
  • KDDI Corporation said attackers exploited a vulnerability in third-party software to access an email system it operates for five Japanese internet service providers, and warned that customer email addresses and passwords may have been obtained.

What Is Agentic Runtime Security? — Optional

Mitiga argues that traditional EDR is no longer enough because modern attacks move through cloud, SaaS, identity, AI agents, and third-party services where endpoint tools have little visibility. Its Agentic Runtime Security offering is positioned as log-based runtime detection and response for stopping active attacks across that broader infrastructure before business impact.

  • Mitiga describes Agentic Runtime Security as runtime detection and response across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services that anticipates, detects, interrupts, and stops active attacks before they reach the business.
  • The article argues that EDR protects endpoints and workloads, while Agentic Runtime Security uses platform audit logs to protect areas where endpoint agents cannot operate.
  • Mitiga says modern attackers move quickly across cloud, SaaS, identity, AI, and third-party services, citing figures such as a 22-second median handoff time for initial access in 2025 and a 29-minute average eCrime breakout time.
  • The article states that Mitiga’s approach depends on a forensic-grade, long-horizon data layer because cloud and service environments do not provide disk images or memory dumps like endpoints do.
  • Mitiga positions its Helios AIDR model around three pillars: Defend with AI, Defend from AI, and Defend the AI, all built on an AI-native foundation.

Webinar: Why traditional email security is no longer enough — Optional

BleepingComputer is hosting a July 8, 2026 webinar on how behavioral AI can help detect and automate response to modern phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks that bypass traditional email security by abusing trusted identities and workflows.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will examine how modern phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks bypass traditional email defenses.
  • The article says many current email attacks impersonate trusted colleagues, vendors, and business partners rather than relying on malicious attachments, known malware, or suspicious domains.
  • The webinar will discuss how behavioral AI can help automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows for email threats.
  • Speakers include Dan Nickolaisen, Solutions Architect Manager at Abnormal AI, and Eric Danneker, Director of Cyber Vigilance and Defense at Novant Health.

Turning Indicators into Intelligence in OpenCTI with Criminal IP — Optional

Criminal IP’s OpenCTI integration enriches IPs, domains, and URLs with reputation scores, infrastructure context, vulnerability links, behavioral signals, and phishing analysis. The goal is to turn raw indicators into connected OpenCTI graph intelligence that helps analysts investigate, correlate, and prioritize threats faster.

  • The Criminal IP integration with OpenCTI enriches IP addresses, domains, and URLs with reputation scoring, infrastructure intelligence, vulnerability data, behavioral signals, and phishing analysis.
  • Criminal IP provides dual-perspective risk scoring for IP addresses, covering both inbound targeting and outbound behavior.
  • The integration structures enriched data as OpenCTI entities and relationships, including CVEs, Autonomous Systems, geolocation, and resolved IP infrastructure.
  • For domains, Criminal IP performs full URL analysis to detect phishing activity, credential harvesting, suspicious files, and impersonation techniques.
  • The article describes SOC triage, threat hunting, infrastructure pivoting, and phishing campaign analysis as key use cases for the integration.

Finding and Addressing Vulnerable and Outdated Web Application Components — Optional

The article emphasizes that outdated or vulnerable web application components are a major security risk and should be continuously inventoried, monitored, and patched. Organizations should use dependency scanning and vulnerability databases to identify risky libraries or frameworks and replace or update them promptly.

  • The article is titled "Finding and Addressing Vulnerable and Outdated Web Application Components."
  • The article focuses on finding vulnerable web application components.
  • The article focuses on addressing outdated web application components.

Microsoft fixes GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel — Optional

Microsoft fixed the broken GIF search in the Windows 11 Emoji Panel for some versions by switching from Tenor to GIPHY in the optional KB5095093 update. The issue still affects Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 while Microsoft works on a fix.

  • Microsoft fixed GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel for Windows 11 users after Google's Tenor GIF search engine retired its API on June 30, 2026.
  • The issue caused some devices to show a message saying "GIF service is not available" instead of GIF options in the Windows Emoji Panel.
  • Microsoft restored the feature for Windows 11 24H2, 25H2, and 26H1 users by switching to GIPHY in the preview KB5095093 Windows cumulative update released on June 23.
  • Microsoft is still working on a fix for Windows 11 23H2 and Windows Server 2025 and has not provided a timeline.
  • The optional KB5095093 update also includes Point-in-Time Restore and fixes a Recycle Bin confirmation dialog bug involving internal file names.

Martin Lee: Running through the Arctic (and the threat landscape) — Optional

Cisco Talos profiles Martin Lee, EMEA Lead, tracing his accidental path from human virus genetics to early internet work and then cybersecurity through spam filtering and early APT detection. His main advice is to stay curious, visible, and open to unexpected career pivots.

  • Martin Lee is the EMEA Lead at Talos, where his work is largely ambassadorial and focused on explaining changes in the threat landscape to customers and partners.
  • Lee began his career researching the genetics of human viruses before leaving academia after discovering the internet early on and deciding it would become a major invention in his lifetime.
  • After the dot-com crash left him with less work than he wanted, Lee took a job writing spam filters before spam filters and spam itself were major problems.
  • While writing spam filters, Lee and his team began detecting unusual attacks that they later understood were among the first APT attacks.
  • The article says Lee’s cybersecurity career began largely by accident and has continued for more than 23 years as the field has grown significantly.

item.title: Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 Access and Rolls Out Claude Sonnet 5
